CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions (augmenting textual features with cognitive gaze-derived features and observed F-score gains) are stated early and carried through to experiments and conclusions. Notation and feature descriptions are mostly clear, and datasets, recording hardware, and evaluation metrics are reported, but the paper omits some implementation details (e.g., hyperparameters, code availability) and could more precisely define the construction of the gaze-saliency graph to aid reproducibility. Figures/tables and examples are reported as effective and writing quality is good, so only minor presentation improvements are needed. | B(4): Based on the provided summary, the paper is well organized (intro → methods → experiments → conclusion) and clearly states its main contributions early (use of gaze-based cognitive features for sentiment). Notation and definitions appear consistent and the writing quality is reported as good, with related work adequately covered; figures/tables and examples seem effective for conveying datasets and results. Minor presentation gaps (e.g., no mention of hyperparameters or code release, and potential loss of detail in aggregated gaze descriptions) slightly reduce clarity but do not hinder overall comprehension.

Figures/tables and examples are reported as effective and writing quality is good, so only minor presentation improvements are needed.

reviewer_b.justification: Based on the provided summary, the paper is well organized (intro → methods → experiments → conclusion) and clearly states its main contributions early (use of gaze-based cognitive features for sentiment). Notation and definitions appear consistent and the writing quality is reported as good, with related work adequately covered; figures/tables and examples seem effective for conveying datasets and results. Minor presentation gaps (e.g., no mention of hyperparameters or code release, and potential loss of detail in aggregated gaze descriptions) slightly reduce clarity but do not hinder overall comprehension.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This paper addresses an applied NLP problem (sentiment classification) using novel cognitive features derived from eye-tracking, which is squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML venues. The methodology — combining textual and gaze-derived features and evaluating with SVM/NB on two datasets — matches venue expectations, but I have concerns about experimental reproducibility and practical impact: hyperparameters are not reported, code is not mentioned, datasets are small and gaze data introduces potential participant/session confounds and hardware-dependent noise. Overall the work is a good fit but would benefit from stronger controls, ablations, and reproducibility artifacts to be a perfect match for a top-tier conference. | B(4): This paper sits squarely within top NLP/ML venues because it addresses sentiment analysis with novel human-signal features (eye-tracking) and reports statistically significant gains on two annotated datasets (Mishra et al., 2016; Joshi et al., 2014) using standard classifiers (SVM/NB) and F-score evaluation. The audience for ACL/EMNLP or computational-cognitive NLP will find the cognitive-feature design and saliency-graph idea relevant, but practical deployment and generalizability concerns (requirement for eye-tracking, hardware/noise limitations, and aggregation losing individual signals) temper its fit and make it more interdisciplinary than core ML. Overall the problem formulation and evaluation meet venue standards, though greater attention to scalability/replicability (code/hyperparameters) and ethical/data-collection implications would strengthen the fit.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper addresses an applied NLP problem (sentiment classification) using novel cognitive features derived from eye-tracking, which is squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML venues. The methodology — combining textual and gaze-derived features and evaluating with SVM/NB on two datasets — matches venue expectations, but I have concerns about experimental reproducibility and practical impact: hyperparameters are not reported, code is not mentioned, datasets are small and gaze data introduces potential participant/session confounds and hardware-dependent noise. Overall the work is a good fit but would benefit from stronger controls, ablations, and reproducibility artifacts to be a perfect match for a top-tier conference.

The audience for ACL/EMNLP or computational-cognitive NLP will find the cognitive-feature design and saliency-graph idea relevant, but practical deployment and generalizability concerns (requirement for eye-tracking, hardware/noise limitations, and aggregation losing individual signals) temper its fit and make it more interdisciplinary than core ML. Overall the problem formulation and evaluation meet venue standards, though greater attention to scalability/replicability (code/hyperparameters) and ethical/data-collection implications would strengthen the fit.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper clearly states its main claim (that eye-tracking derived cognitive features improve sentiment classification) and supplies experimental results on two eye-tracking datasets showing F-score gains; however, key methodological details needed to fully assess correctness are missing (no hyperparameters, no code, limited description of statistical tests beyond claiming significance). There are potential confounds and reproducibility issues I would expect a rigorous evaluation to address: small numbers of annotators/readers per dataset, different eye-tracker hardware and sampling rates, aggregation of participant gaze graphs that can induce information loss, and no explicit controls for reader- or session-level leakage. Given these gaps the empirical claims are plausible but not yet fully substantiated, so the paper merits cautious acceptance pending more complete experimental and statistical reporting. | B(4): The paper’s main claims are clearly articulated and supported by experimental comparisons on two eye-tracking datasets (Mishra et al., 2016; Joshi et al., 2014) showing reported F‑score gains and statements of statistical significance. There are no mathematical derivations to check, and the methodological setup uses sensible baselines (textual features, SVM/NB and a rule‑based MPQA system), but important experimental details are missing (no hyperparameter reporting, unclear which statistical tests were used) and the small number of annotators/participants and differing eye‑tracker hardware raise concerns about generalizability. The authors acknowledge key limitations (need for gaze data, aggregation loss), so while the results appear technically sound overall, these omissions and potential confounds justify a minor‑issues rating rather than perfection.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper clearly states its main claim (that eye-tracking derived cognitive features improve sentiment classification) and supplies experimental results on two eye-tracking datasets showing F-score gains; however, key methodological details needed to fully assess correctness are missing (no hyperparameters, no code, limited description of statistical tests beyond claiming significance). There are potential confounds and reproducibility issues I would expect a rigorous evaluation to address: small numbers of annotators/readers per dataset, different eye-tracker hardware and sampling rates, aggregation of participant gaze graphs that can induce information loss, and no explicit controls for reader- or session-level leakage. Given these gaps the empirical claims are plausible but not yet fully substantiated, so the paper merits cautious acceptance pending more complete experimental and statistical reporting.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper’s main claims are clearly articulated and supported by experimental comparisons on two eye-tracking datasets (Mishra et al., 2016; Joshi et al., 2014) showing reported F‑score gains and statements of statistical significance. There are no mathematical derivations to check, and the methodological setup uses sensible baselines (textual features, SVM/NB and a rule‑based MPQA system), but important experimental details are missing (no hyperparameter reporting, unclear which statistical tests were used) and the small number of annotators/participants and differing eye‑tracker hardware raise concerns about generalizability. The authors acknowledge key limitations (need for gaze data, aggregation loss), so while the results appear technically sound overall, these omissions and potential confounds justify a minor‑issues rating rather than perfection.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against sensible baselines (text-only systems, an MPQA-based rule baseline, SVM and Naive Bayes) on the same two eye-tracking annotated datasets and uses the same evaluation metrics (F-score, accuracy), so most comparisons are fair and reproducible in principle. The authors also perform feature-significance analysis to attribute gains to the cognitive features. However, the experimental scope omits stronger or more recent state-of-the-art sentiment models (e.g., neural classifiers) and hyperparameter/configuration details are not reported, which weakens claims of superiority. Overall the comparisons are solid but not fully comprehensive given current baselines and missing implementation detail. | B(4): The paper evaluates its approach against reasonable and clearly described baselines (textual-feature systems, an MPQA-based rule system, SVM and Naive Bayes) using the same datasets and F-score/accuracy metrics, so comparisons appear fair. It analyzes differences via feature-significance tests and reports dataset/inter-annotator details, and it acknowledges practical limitations of gaze data. However, as a scholar in the area I note missing comparisons to more recent or stronger sentiment/sarcasm models (e.g., contemporary neural or specialized sarcasm detectors), so while the experimental comparison is solid it omits some relevant state-of-the-art baselines.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against sensible baselines (text-only systems, an MPQA-based rule baseline, SVM and Naive Bayes) on the same two eye-tracking annotated datasets and uses the same evaluation metrics (F-score, accuracy), so most comparisons are fair and reproducible in principle. The authors also perform feature-significance analysis to attribute gains to the cognitive features. However, the experimental scope omits stronger or more recent state-of-the-art sentiment models (e.g., neural classifiers) and hyperparameter/configuration details are not reported, which weakens claims of superiority. Overall the comparisons are solid but not fully comprehensive given current baselines and missing implementation detail.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper evaluates its approach against reasonable and clearly described baselines (textual-feature systems, an MPQA-based rule system, SVM and Naive Bayes) using the same datasets and F-score/accuracy metrics, so comparisons appear fair. It analyzes differences via feature-significance tests and reports dataset/inter-annotator details, and it acknowledges practical limitations of gaze data. However, as a scholar in the area I note missing comparisons to more recent or stronger sentiment/sarcasm models (e.g., contemporary neural or specialized sarcasm detectors), so while the experimental comparison is solid it omits some relevant state-of-the-art baselines.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): This paper presents a useful proof-of-concept that cognitive (eye‑tracking) features can measurably improve sentiment classification (reported up to 3.7% and 9.3% F‑score gains on two datasets) and introduces concrete gaze‑saliency graph features that can seed follow‑on work. However, the requirement for eye‑tracking data, modest dataset sizes (994 and 843 examples) and lack of reported reproducibility details (no code or hyperparameters) limit immediate practical uptake by researchers and practitioners. Overall, the work opens promising new directions at the intersection of cognitive signals and NLP but is moderate in impact until scalability, replication, and deployment constraints are further addressed. | B(3): The paper tackles an important challenge—improving sentiment classification for complex phenomena like sarcasm—by introducing eye-tracking derived cognitive features and demonstrating meaningful F-score gains (up to 3.7% and 9.3% on two datasets recorded with Eyelink-1000 and Tobii T120). This work is likely to shift researcher thinking about the value of human cognitive signals and open new interdisciplinary lines (gaze-informed NLP, multimodal annotation) but its real-world impact is constrained by practical barriers (need for eye-tracking hardware, aggregation losses) that limit immediate broad adoption. Overall, the contribution is a useful, novel direction with moderate impact rather than a field-changing advance.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper presents a useful proof-of-concept that cognitive (eye‑tracking) features can measurably improve sentiment classification (reported up to 3.7% and 9.3% F‑score gains on two datasets) and introduces concrete gaze‑saliency graph features that can seed follow‑on work. However, the requirement for eye‑tracking data, modest dataset sizes (994 and 843 examples) and lack of reported reproducibility details (no code or hyperparameters) limit immediate practical uptake by researchers and practitioners. Overall, the work opens promising new directions at the intersection of cognitive signals and NLP but is moderate in impact until scalability, replication, and deployment constraints are further addressed.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper tackles an important challenge—improving sentiment classification for complex phenomena like sarcasm—by introducing eye-tracking derived cognitive features and demonstrating meaningful F-score gains (up to 3.7% and 9.3% on two datasets recorded with Eyelink-1000 and Tobii T120). This work is likely to shift researcher thinking about the value of human cognitive signals and open new interdisciplinary lines (gaze-informed NLP, multimodal annotation) but its real-world impact is constrained by practical barriers (need for eye-tracking hardware, aggregation losses) that limit immediate broad adoption. Overall, the contribution is a useful, novel direction with moderate impact rather than a field-changing advance.

REPLICABILITY.justification: [Consensus avg=2] A(2): Important experimental details are missing: hyperparameters and model training configurations are explicitly not reported, and the preprocessing pipeline for text and gaze features (including aggregation and handling of participant-level variance) is not clearly described. While the two datasets are cited with hardware and participant counts, no code is provided and critical implementation details for gaze-saliency graph construction and feature extraction are under-specified, making faithful reimplementation difficult. Evaluation metrics are reported (F-score, accuracy, Fleiss κ), but that alone is insufficient to ensure reproducibility. | B(3): The paper specifies datasets used (two prior corpora with recording hardware and participant counts) and reports evaluation metrics (F-score, accuracy, Fleiss kappa), so core resources and evaluation procedures are clear. However, important experimental details are missing: hyperparameters and model configuration details are not reported, and the preprocessing/feature-extraction pipeline for eye-tracking (including exact aggregation and graph-construction parameters) is only sketchily described. Code is not provided nor mentioned, which means reimplementation would require nontrivial reconstruction of gaze-feature engineering from the text. From a replicability standpoint this yields a partially reproducible study with some important details absent.

reviewer_a.justification: Important experimental details are missing: hyperparameters and model training configurations are explicitly not reported, and the preprocessing pipeline for text and gaze features (including aggregation and handling of participant-level variance) is not clearly described. While the two datasets are cited with hardware and participant counts, no code is provided and critical implementation details for gaze-saliency graph construction and feature extraction are under-specified, making faithful reimplementation difficult. Evaluation metrics are reported (F-score, accuracy, Fleiss κ), but that alone is insufficient to ensure reproducibility.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper specifies datasets used (two prior corpora with recording hardware and participant counts) and reports evaluation metrics (F-score, accuracy, Fleiss kappa), so core resources and evaluation procedures are clear. However, important experimental details are missing: hyperparameters and model configuration details are not reported, and the preprocessing/feature-extraction pipeline for eye-tracking (including exact aggregation and graph-construction parameters) is only sketchily described. Code is not provided nor mentioned, which means reimplementation would require nontrivial reconstruction of gaze-feature engineering from the text. From a replicability standpoint this yields a partially reproducible study with some important details absent.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper introduces a noteworthy and not-obvious idea: augmenting sentiment classifiers with eye-tracking–derived cognitive features (including novel gaze-saliency graph features) which, to the authors’ claim, has not been applied to sentiment analysis before. It clearly differentiates itself from prior textual-only sentiment and sarcasm work by integrating fixation/saccade/regression statistics and graph-based features and reports empirical gains, indicating a non-trivial extension beyond existing methods. From an expert perspective this is a notable and informative cross-disciplinary contribution, though it is not a paradigm shift given the reliance on auxiliary eye-tracking data and builds on established classification frameworks rather than proposing fundamentally new learning algorithms. | B(4): The paper brings a notable and fairly novel perspective by introducing eye-tracking–derived cognitive features (basic gaze statistics and graph-based saliency features) into sentiment polarity classification—an approach the authors claim is the first of its kind. It clearly differentiates itself from traditional text-only baselines and shows non-trivial methodological additions (graph-based gaze features) with empirical F-score gains on two datasets. From my domain perspective this is more than a routine combination of known techniques, though it is not a radical theoretical advance; practical limitations (need for eye-tracking data) temper how surprising the contribution would be to experts.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper introduces a noteworthy and not-obvious idea: augmenting sentiment classifiers with eye-tracking–derived cognitive features (including novel gaze-saliency graph features) which, to the authors’ claim, has not been applied to sentiment analysis before. It clearly differentiates itself from prior textual-only sentiment and sarcasm work by integrating fixation/saccade/regression statistics and graph-based features and reports empirical gains, indicating a non-trivial extension beyond existing methods. From an expert perspective this is a notable and informative cross-disciplinary contribution, though it is not a paradigm shift given the reliance on auxiliary eye-tracking data and builds on established classification frameworks rather than proposing fundamentally new learning algorithms.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper brings a notable and fairly novel perspective by introducing eye-tracking–derived cognitive features (basic gaze statistics and graph-based saliency features) into sentiment polarity classification—an approach the authors claim is the first of its kind. It clearly differentiates itself from traditional text-only baselines and shows non-trivial methodological additions (graph-based gaze features) with empirical F-score gains on two datasets. From my domain perspective this is more than a routine combination of known techniques, though it is not a radical theoretical advance; practical limitations (need for eye-tracking data) temper how surprising the contribution would be to experts.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper provides reasonable evidence (two eye-tracking datasets with different hardware and multiple classifiers) that adding gaze-derived features can improve sentiment classification and includes feature-significance analysis to attribute gains. However, I see gaps: there is no mention of hyperparameters, random seeds, or code, limiting reproducibility; ablation/sensitivity analysis appears partial (feature significance reported but full controlled ablations and per-feature effect sizes with statistical testing are not fully described). Related work coverage and discussion of limitations are adequate, but experimental design details (preprocessing, training/validation splits, handling of participant/session-level confounds) are insufficient for rigorous replication. | B(4): Experiments span two independently recorded datasets, multiple classifiers (SVM, NB, rule-based) and compare traditional versus cognitively augmented feature sets, which provides solid empirical support for the main claim; they also report feature significance analysis. However, key reproducibility details are missing (no hyperparameters, code), and sensitivity analyses around aggregation of participant gaze, per-participant effects, and robustness to eye-tracker noise are not reported—these are important given the work’s reliance on gaze data. Related work coverage is said to be adequate and limitations (hardware, data availability, aggregation loss) are acknowledged, so overall the evaluation is strong but with notable gaps that prevent it from being fully comprehensive.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper provides reasonable evidence (two eye-tracking datasets with different hardware and multiple classifiers) that adding gaze-derived features can improve sentiment classification and includes feature-significance analysis to attribute gains. However, I see gaps: there is no mention of hyperparameters, random seeds, or code, limiting reproducibility; ablation/sensitivity analysis appears partial (feature significance reported but full controlled ablations and per-feature effect sizes with statistical testing are not fully described). Related work coverage and discussion of limitations are adequate, but experimental design details (preprocessing, training/validation splits, handling of participant/session-level confounds) are insufficient for rigorous replication.

reviewer_b.justification: Experiments span two independently recorded datasets, multiple classifiers (SVM, NB, rule-based) and compare traditional versus cognitively augmented feature sets, which provides solid empirical support for the main claim; they also report feature significance analysis. However, key reproducibility details are missing (no hyperparameters, code), and sensitivity analyses around aggregation of participant gaze, per-participant effects, and robustness to eye-tracker noise are not reported—these are important given the work’s reliance on gaze data. Related work coverage is said to be adequate and limitations (hardware, data availability, aggregation loss) are acknowledged, so overall the evaluation is strong but with notable gaps that prevent it from being fully comprehensive.

main_claims: Augmenting traditional textual features with cognitive features derived from readers' eye-movement patterns improves sentiment polarity classification.

The augmented feature set yields statistically significant improvements (up to 3.7% and 9.3% F-score gains on two datasets) over systems using only traditional features, and particularly helps handle complex constructs like sarcasm, irony and thwarting.

They introduce a set of cognitive gaze-based features (basic gaze statistics and graph-based saliency features) for sentiment analysis and show via feature significance analysis that these features contribute to improved performance.
This approach is presented as the first work (to the authors' knowledge) to use eye-tracking derived cognitive features to empower sentiment analyzers.
_summary.methodology: Combine traditional sentiment and sarcasm-related textual features with cognitive features extracted from eye-tracking (fixations, saccades, regressions, and gaze-saliency graph features), then perform statistical classification using classifiers (SVM, Naive Bayes and an in-house rule-based classifier) and compare performance across feature combinations.
datasets: Dataset 1 (Mishra et al., 2016) — 994 snippets, annotated by 7 participants, recorded with SR-Research Eyelink-1000 (500Hz)
Dataset 2 (Joshi et al., 2014) — 1059 snippets (843 after removing objective), annotated by 5 participants, recorded with Tobii T120 (120Hz)
baselines: Systems using only traditional textual features
In-house rule-based (RB) classifier based on MPQA counts (with handling of negators and intensifiers)
metrics: Fleiss kappa (inter-annotator agreement reported for datasets)
limitations: Requires availability of eye-tracking (gaze) data for readers, limiting practicality.
Eye-tracker hardware limitations can cause errors in measurements.
Aggregating eye-tracking parameters (e.g., averaging participant graphs) may cause loss of individual gaze information and reduce effectiveness.

reviewer_a.name: Methodological Rigourist
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer with 10+ years designing and evaluating NLP experiments in industry and academia; focuses on reproducible pipelines and robust statistical evaluation of classification systems.
traits: insists on clear, reproducible experimental setup (data splits, preprocessing, hyperparameters, random seeds, code/compute details)
valuates thorough ablation studies and proper baselines to attribute gains to specific features or components
looks for appropriate statistical testing and effect-size reporting rather than only pointwise improvements
notices potential leakage or confounds (e.g., participant- or session-level correlations in eye-tracking data) and requests controls
expects careful feature engineering descriptions and implementation details for nontrivial features (e.g., gaze-saliency graph construction)
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Carefully probes experimental design and evidence, demanding clarity and reproducibility so claims about performance and feature contributions are well supported.

reviewer_b.name: Domain & Impact Scholar
reviewer_b.background: Professor of NLP and Cognitive Modeling who studies sentiment, sarcasm detection, and human-in-the-loop language technologies; tracks the literature on leveraging user signals and cognitive data for language understanding.
traits: assesses novelty relative to prior work in sentiment analysis and human-signal augmented NLP, checking whether contributions fill a real gap
values clarity about practical applicability, scalability, and the conditions under which cognitive features are useful
attends to ethical, privacy, and data-collection implications of using eye-tracking and whether alternatives exist
examines whether proposed methods advance understanding (e.g., interpretability of why gaze features help) beyond incremental performance gains
notices connections to cognitive theory and whether the feature design is motivated by prior psycholinguistic findings
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Evaluates novelty, broader significance, and real-world feasibility, balancing appreciation for interdisciplinary ideas with scrutiny of impact and ethical considerations.
